segments.s631-g41: همی is an old narrative adverb marking a continuous or emphatic action; here it frames the verb زد. بر زمین زد is verb + object: زد (past tense 'struck') followed by the place complement بر زمین ('upon the ground'). چنان ('so/so much') introduces the result clause that follows. کاستخوانش is a compound referring to the person's bones/skull attached by the possessive -ش and links as the object affected by شکست in the next segment.
segments.s631-g42: شکست is a past-tense result verb here meaning 'was broken' (breakage). The coordinating و links the result to a second effect. بپالود is an old third-person past prefixed form (بـ- + پالود) meaning 'caused to be cleared/purified' or 'stripped away' and takes رنگ رخانش ('the color of his face/complexion') as its object; رخانش is a descriptive/possessive phrase (رخان + -ش). Together the clause describes a further visible consequence of the blow.
segments.s631-g43: بدو is an indirect object adverb meaning 'to him' (i.e., 'for him' / 'he was left'). مانده is the past participle 'remained/was left'. بد shنگل is a descriptive phrase: بد 'bad/terrible' modifies شنگل (an obscure/poetic noun here meaning a confusion/daze or similar), and اندر شگفت ('in astonishment') states the emotional condition. Word order is subjectless/resultative: 'to him remained a terrible shengel, in wonder.'
segments.s631-g44: ازان 'from that/of that' introduces the sources of his amazement. برز بالا is a doubled expression emphasizing loftiness ('high, exalted, lofty'), و آن زور و کفت links two more particulars: زور 'strength/might' and کفت (here likely 'کف', the palm/striking hand). The sequence lists the features that caused his astonishment.
segments.s631-g45: به هندی here marks the language or style ('in Indian'). همی again gives a habitual/epic narrative flavor before the verb. نام یزدان بخواند is a causative/active past: بخواند (archaic past of خواندن) here means 'he named/called (him)'; نام یزدان is the name given ('Yazdān'). Word order is adverbial phrase → object (name) → verb.
segments.s631-g46: ورا is the indirect object 'to him' (i.e., 'he made/appointed for him'). از marks the comparison/source ('over/from forty men'), مرد here means 'men/warriors' (contextually 'valiant men'), برتر is an adjective 'superior/better', and نشاند is an archaic transitive past 'set/placed/caused to sit' (i.e., 'he seated him above'). The whole clause means he raised/placed him above forty men.
segments.s631-g47: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. گشتند is plural past 'they became/turned' (here 'became drunk'), modifying the predicate مست 'drunk'. از introduces the cause/instrument ('from/of') and می‌خوشگوار is a compound meaning 'pleasant/delightful wine' (می 'wine' + خوشگوار 'pleasant'). The clause states the condition leading to departure.
segments.s631-g48: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed/went away'. ز (poetic variant of از) marks motion away from the location. ایوان 'iwan/porch' is the place, and گوهرنگار is a compound adjective 'jeweled/gem-encrusted' describing the iwan. The clause completes the action begun in the first half.
segments.s631-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is 3rd-person singular past 'he heard'; شنگل is the subject/name 'Shangal'; به + بهرام is a prepositional phrase 'to Bahram'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'. Together this clause sets up reported speech introduced by the following که.
segments.s631-g2: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content of what was said; رای تو is a genitive phrase 'your counsel/opinion' (رای here = counsel/opinion); با + مردمی frames a comparison/compatibility 'with the people'; نیست is the negative copula 'is not'; جفت functions as predicate 'paired/agreeing' (literally 'not paired with the people').
segments.s631-g3: زمانی is an adverb 'for a while/for a time'; فرودآی is an imperative/hortative 'come down' (classical form with -آی); و is the coordinating 'and'; بگشای is an imperative 'open/unfasten' (بـ- + گشای); بند is the direct object 'bond/fastening' (a cord, curtain or tie).
segments.s631-g4: چه گویی is an interrogative phrase 'what/why do you say' (چه here asks about the speaker's words); سخن‌های is the plural 'words/speeches'; ناسودمند is an adjective 'unprofitable/useless'. The clause questions the addressee's utterances as fruitless.
segments.s631-g5: یکی here is 'one' or 'a certain' (introducing a single iwan); خرم is an adjective 'pleasant/joyful' describing the ایوان (iwan/porch); ایوان is the noun 'iwan/porch/hall'; بپرداختند is a past-tense verb 'they paid/attended to/treated' — in this context it means they set up or completed/constructed that iwan.
segments.s631-g6: همه 'everyone/all'; هرچ is an indefinite 'whatever/whatever was needed'; بایست is a modal/necessitative here 'ought/needed to' (classical usage meaning 'was necessary'); برساختند is the past plural 'they built/constructed'. Together: everyone built whatever had to be built.
segments.s631-g7: بیاسود is a negative participial/adjectival form 'not eased/uneasy/did not rest'; placed before the name it describes the subject's state; بهرام is the subject 'Bahram'; تا introduces a temporal limit 'until'; نیم‌روز is 'midday/noon'. The phrase says Bahram remained unrestful up to noon.
segments.s631-g8: چو is the conjunction 'when'; بر + اوج شد is a verbal phrase 'rose to the apex' (بر = on/upon, اوج = peak, شد = became/rose); تاج گیتی فروز is a descriptive noun phrase 'the world's shining/bright crown' (فروز = shining/bright) which is what reaches the apex. The clause gives the temporal point when the preceding state ends.
segments.s631-g57: به + name marks the indirect object: 'to Bahram'. فرمود is a past-tense reporting verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate/purposive clause here ('that/so that') and بر نشست is a compound where بر (on/upon) + نشست ('sit/mount') together mean 'to sit upon' or 'to mount' — a volitional/result clause triggered by تا after فرمود.
segments.s631-g58: This is a participial/nominal phrase describing what he has in hand: کمان کیانی = 'the Kayanian (royal) bow' (epithet after the noun, poetic order). گرفته is the past participle 'taken/seized' and with the following prepositional phrase به دست ('in hand') yields 'having taken the Kayanian bow in his hand.'
segments.s631-g59: به شنگل = 'to Shengel' (indirect object). چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' and گفت is the past 'he said'; کای شهریار is a vocative ('O king') inserted as the form of address to whom the forthcoming statement is directed. The clause order is poetic but reads as 'he said thus to Shengel: "O king..."'.
segments.s631-g60: چنان دان is an exhortative/imperative turn: دان here means 'know'/'consider' (i.e. 'be aware of this'). که introduces the content clause 'that ...', هستند is the plural present/past-form 'they are', and با من سوار places the predicate complement 'riders with me' after the verb — a poetic word order meaning 'that the riders are with me.'
segments.s631-g61: همی is a habitual/continuous adverb 'continually/always'. تیر و چوگان is a coordinated object 'arrows and (the) polo [game/mallet]'. کنند is the (3rd pl.) present/subjunctive 'they do/make' used here for habitual or potential action. آرزوی follows the verb as an object/adverbial meaning 'by desire / at will / for pleasure' — 'they do arrows and polo at will.'
segments.s631-g62: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. فرمان دهد is a clause 'gives the command/order' where دهد is present/subjunctive/irrealis form 'gives' used in the time clause. شاه آزاده‌خوی is the noun plus epithet 'the magnanimous king' (adjective after the noun in poetic word order).
segments.s631-g63: چنین گفت شنگل = 'Shengel said thus' (چنین 'thus', گفت past 'said', شنگل subject). که introduces the reported-content clause 'that ...', and تیر و کمان is the coordinated subject/object 'arrows and bow' starting that clause.
segments.s631-g64: ستون سواران is a genitival phrase 'the pillar/support of the riders' (in full prose it would be ستونِ سواران). بود is the past copula 'was/were', and بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' modifying the clause. Together they state 'were the pillar of the riders, undoubtedly.'
segments.s631-g65: This is a nominal phrase describing the addressee: تو (you) + با (with) introduces the instruments/features 'شاخ و یالی' (horn and mane). بیفراز دست is an adjectival/participial phrase meaning 'with the hand unraised' or 'keeping the hand low' (بیفراز = 'unraised/low' modifying دست). Word order is poetic: attributes follow the preposition phrase and qualify the subject.
segments.s631-g66: This is a sequence of imperatives/instructions. به زه کن کمان را literally places the prepositional phrase به زه ('to/onto the string') before the imperative کن ('do' = 'string' here), while کمان را is the definite object marked by را and comes immediately after the verb phrase in poetic order. و introduces the second command: بگشای (imperative 'open, spread') takes شست ('thumb') as its object (no را), so the two verbs form coordinated commands: string the bow and spread the thumb.
segments.s631-g67: کمان را (the bow, definite by را) is the direct object; به زه کرد is a light-verb construction with به زه ('to/onto the string') + کرد (past of کردن 'to do/make'), so the clause means 'he strung the bow.' The personal name بَهرام follows and گرد here functions as an epithet/title attached to the name in the poetic sequence.
segments.s631-g68: This clause uses the same accusative marking pattern: عنان را (the reins, object + را) + به (to) + اسپ تگاور (a steed described by تگاور). سپرد is the past tense of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over', so the whole segment means 'he entrusted the reins to the (war-)steed.' The preposition به introduces the recipient.
segments.s631-g69: یکی is an indefinite numeral/particle 'one' meaning 'one of them' and functions as subject. تیر is the object 'an arrow' and بگرفت is the past tense ('took/seized') — the verbal form has the archaic b- prefix common in classical Persian (بگرفت). The coordinated clause و بگشاد شست uses بگشاد (past of گشودن 'to open') with شست 'thumb' as its object: 'and opened/ spread his thumb.'
segments.s631-g70: نشانه here functions as the target/mark of archery (a signed/aimed object). به یک چوبه places that target 'to a single post/shaft' (چوبه = beam/post). بر هم شکست is a verbal phrase where بر (on/upon) + هم (together/one another) + شکست (broke) yields 'it struck the post and broke' or 'was broken upon it' — the particle هم gives the sense of coming together/impact.
segments.s631-g71: گرفتند is a past-tense verb 'they took/raised' (here 'they raised a shout'). یکسر is an adverb meaning 'at once/altogether' modifying the action. برو آفرین is an idiomatic exclamatory phrase — بُرو (literally 'go') combines with آفرین ('bravo/praise') to form a shouted acclamation ('Bravo!'); in poetry such verbal elements function as interjectional formulae rather than literal commands.
segments.s631-g72: This is a noun phrase in apposition naming who shouted: سواران میدان = 'the horsemen of the field/arena' and مردان کین = 'the men of vengeance' (مردان modified by کین). The coordination with و links the two groups; these nouns are the logical subjects/agents of the preceding verb 'گرفتند.'
segments.s631-g49: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'); گردون is the subject 'the heavens/firmament'. بپوشید is a classical past 3rd‑person singular (ب- prefix + پوشید) meaning 'put on' or 'clothed itself with'. چینی حریر is the object phrase 'Chinese silk' (چینی as an adjective 'Chinese', حریر 'silk'), following the verb in this poetic inversion.
segments.s631-g50: ز here means 'from' or 'after' introducing the source/cause ('from eating'); خوردن is the verbal noun 'eating'. برآسود is a past/static predicative ('was at rest' / 'had repose'). برنا و پیر is the coordinated subject 'young and old' placed after the predicate (classical Persian often allows this word order).
segments.s631-g51: چو 'when' starts a temporal clause. زرین شد is a two‑word verbal phrase 'became golden' (زرین: adjective 'golden', شد: past 'became'). آن چادر مشکبوی is the noun phrase 'that musk‑scented tent' (آن 'that' + چادر 'tent/veil' + مشکبوی 'musk‑scented'), placed after the predicate in poetic word order.
segments.s631-g52: فروزنده is a nominative epithet 'the radiant/illuminating one' (likely the sun or a bright presence). بر چرخ is a locative phrase 'upon the sky/heaven' (بر + چرخ). بنمود is the past 3rd‑person singular 'showed, displayed', and روی is the direct object 'face' — together 'displayed its face upon the sky.'
segments.s631-g53: شه هندوان is a genitive/construct phrase 'the king of the Indians' (شه 'king' + هندوان 'Indians'). باره را has the object marker را marking 'باره' (here 'باره' = load/litter/bench in context, i.e. what he sat upon). برنشست is past 3rd‑person singular 'sat upon / took his seat on.' The verb follows the object phrase in this poetic ordering.
segments.s631-g54: به میدان is a directional phrase 'into/onto the field/arena' (به = towards/into, میدان = field/arena). خرامید is the past 3rd‑person singular 'strode/pranced proudly'. چوگان به دست is a postpositional instrumental phrase 'with a polo‑mallet in (his) hand' (چوگان 'polo mallet', به = by/with, دست 'hand'), modifying the action.
segments.s631-g55: ببردند is past 3rd‑person plural 'they took/carried/brought'. با here links the items to the king (classical usage allows ambiguity: either 'with the king' or 'the king's ...'); تیر و کمان is the coordinated object 'arrows and bow' (تیر 'arrow(s)', کمان 'bow'). The whole phrase means they handled/transported the king's arrows and bow.
segments.s631-g56: همی is an adverbial particle giving a continuous or immediate sense ('still/was just'); تاخت is past 3rd‑person singular 'galloped/charged'. بر آرزو is a prepositional phrase 'toward/against آرزو' — here آرزو is used figuratively as 'desire/aim' (i.e. he rode toward his aim). یک زمان is an adverbial 'for a moment/for an instant' modifying the verb.
segments.s631-g17: چو + past clause = temporal 'when'. نان خورده شد is a passive construction: خورده (past participle 'eaten') + شد (past auxiliary 'to be'), so 'the bread was eaten'. مجلس آراستند is a simple past (third person plural) meaning 'they arranged/adorned the assembly' — آراستند is a past form used here to mean 'set/arranged'.
segments.s631-g18: نوازنده رود is a noun phrase 'the player of the rud' (نوازنده = musician, رود = the string instrument rud). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. می‌خواستند is imperfect/past habitual (third plural) 'they wanted / desired' — here it indicates the guests' wish for music or that they intended something to follow.
segments.s631-g19: همی before a past verb gives a continuing/iterative sense ('kept / continuously'). بوی مشک آمد is a straightforward clause 'the scent of musk came' (آمد = 'came'). از + خوردنی = 'from the food' where خوردنی is used as a noun meaning 'food/thing to be eaten'.
segments.s631-g20: همان زیر = 'that very/that same beneath' (همان = 'that'), زربفت گستردنی describes the covering: زربفت (gold-brocade) + گستردنی, here functioning as a participial/adjectival form meaning 'that was spread / laid out' (poetic usage). Literally 'beneath that spread of gold-brocade.'
segments.s631-g21: بزرگان 'the great ones / nobles' is the subject. چو + از باده خرم شدند = 'when (from) wine they became joyful' — از باده indicates cause ('because/of wine'), خرم is a predicate adjective 'joyful', and شدند is past plural 'they became'.
segments.s631-g22: ز تیمار نابوده is a short poetic clause: ز (poetic 'from'/'out of') تیمار 'care/worry' نابوده (past participle) 'was undone/annihilated' — meaning their cares were removed. بی‌غم شدند is 'they became carefree' (بی‌غم = 'without sorrow/care', شدند = past plural 'became').
segments.s631-g23: دو تن 'two persons' (تن used as 'person'). را is the direct-object marker attached to the phrase. بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb here meaning 'he commanded/ordered/said (to do something)' — in classical style used for issuing an order. زورآزمای is used as a noun/epithet 'tester of strength / strongman, champion' describing the role he summoned them for.
segments.s631-g24: به کشتی = 'to wrestling' (به + noun = 'to/for [the contest of]'), where کشتی means 'wrestling' here. که دارند introduces a relative clause 'who have / who are to have' (دارند = 'they have/are to engage in'). با دیو پای = 'with the demon-footed one' (با = 'with', دیو = 'demon/monster', پای = 'foot'); the whole tail means they are to wrestle against a demon-footed opponent.
segments.s631-g9: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. در پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before' (در + پیش) that marks the location of the action. نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed/laid', and خوان is its direct object 'the banquet / table'; the verb comes after the locative phrase and the object in this poetic arrangement.
segments.s631-g10: یکی را is marked by را as the (definite/particular) object 'someone' (here: 'one person'). بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/ordered'. کو را بخوان is reported interrogative speech: کو (here interrogative 'who/where is') plus را (emphatic/definite) and بخوان (the verb 'to sing/recite' in the present/imperative sense used as the reported command).
segments.s631-g11: کز is a relative/particle 'who/that/from' introducing the origin clause; ایران is the source 'Iran'; فرستاده is a noun 'envoy/messenger' (here 'sent one'); خسروپرست is a compound adjective 'Khosrow-worshipping' modifying the envoy.
segments.s631-g12: This is a nominal predication: سخن‌گوی 'speaker/one who speaks' is listed, joined by و هم 'and also' to کامگار 'successful/one who attains wishes'. نوست is an adjectival/postpositive element (classical form meaning 'is new' or 'is...') used here as a descriptive ending; together the phrase describes qualities of the envoy.
segments.s631-g13: کسی را is the object phrase 'someone' marked by را. که با اوست is a relative clause 'who is with him' (که introducing the clause, با 'with', او 'him', ست copula 'is'). هم زین‌نشان 'also zinneshan' adds an additional predicate 'also bearing a saddle / mounted' describing that same person.
segments.s631-g14: بیاور is an imperative 'bring'. به خوان رسولان is the directional phrase 'to the messengers' table' (به + خوان + رسولان as genitive construction). نشان here functions as 'a token/sign' or 'one bearing the sign' — the item/person to be brought.
segments.s631-g15: بشد تیز is a compact past construction: بشد ('it happened / he became') + تیز used adverbially 'swift/keen', so 'he became swift' or 'he moved quickly'. بهرام is the subject, و joins the clauses, and بر خوان نشست is a simple past clause 'sat upon the table' (بر 'on' + خوان 'table' + نشست 'sat').
segments.s631-g16: This clause lists small physical actions: بنان دست 'the fingertips of the hand' is the noun phrase, followed by بگشاد, here a past verb 'he opened/spread' (opened his fingers). و links the next action; لب را ببست is 'he closed his lips' with را marking لب as definite object and ببست the past verb 'he shut/closed'.
segments.s631-g33: چو at the clause start is a temporal/conditional conjunction ('when'/'if'). با زورمندان is a prepositional phrase ('with the powerful'); the verb phrase به کشتی شوم places the preposition به + کشتی ('into wrestling/fighting') before the verb شوم. شوم is the first‑person subjunctive/potential ('I may become' / 'I become'), used here as a habitual/predictive statement about the speaker.
segments.s631-g34: نه negates the following clause. اندر is an archaic preposition meaning 'in/within', governing the nouns خرابی و مستی ('ruin and drunkenness'). شوم repeats the same verb form as above ('I become'), so the whole phrase means 'I will not become in ruin and drunkenness' (i.e. 'I will not be ruined or drunk').
segments.s631-g35: بخندید is a past third‑person verb 'laughed' (poetic V–S order). شنگل is the actor/name. بدو گفت is a common sequence meaning 'said to him' (بد = to + او contracted), and خیز is the imperative 'rise!' addressed to the addressee.
segments.s631-g36: This is the reported command. چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when' (or 'if'). زیر آوری is a causative/resultive combination: زیر ('under/subject to') + آوری (you bring) meaning 'you bring under/subdue'. خون ایشان بریز is the imperative 'spill their blood' (خون direct object, ایشان 'their/them', بریز imperative).
segments.s631-g37: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. بهرام is the subject. بر پای خاست is an idiom ('stood upon his feet' = 'stood up'): بر (on/upon) + پای (foot) + خاست (arose). Poetic word order places the verb(s) around the subject.
segments.s631-g38: به مردی is an adverbial/ethical phrase 'with manliness' (مردی = manliness/valour) introduced by به used adverbially. خم آورد is a light‑verb construction: خم ('bend') + آورد ('brought/caused'), meaning 'bent' or 'arched'. بالای راست is the directional/locative phrase 'to the upper/right side' (بالای = upper/above, راست = to the right), describing the direction of the bend.
segments.s631-g39: کسی را is the indefinite object 'someone' with the definite/object marker را. که introduces a relative clause 'whom/that'. بگرفت is the past tense 'he seized/took'. ز ایشان میان = 'from among them / in their midst' (زِ = from, ایشان = them, میان = among/in the middle). The whole segment forms 'someone whom he seized from among them.'
segments.s631-g40: چو here is a simile marker 'as/like'. شیری is used leonine/nominally ('a lion' / 'like a lion'). که introduces the subordinate clause describing that lion: یازد is an archaic verb 'bursts forth / breaks open', and به گور ژیان literally 'into the grave of the living' (به = into/toward, گور = grave, ژیان = life/the living), a poetic image meaning a violent onslaught that sends the living to the grave.
segments.s631-g25: برفتند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb ('they went/left') that opens the clause; the nominal phrase شایسته مردان کار follows and describes who and for what — 'شایسته' (an adjective) qualifies 'مردان' ('men'), and 'کار' is an object/goal ('task/work') attached after the group. In classical Persian the verb can appear before its subject phrase for emphasis or poetic rhythm.
segments.s631-g26: ببستندشان is the past 3rd-person plural form 'they bound/tied' with the enclitic object pronoun شان = 'them' attached; بر is the preposition 'on/upon', and میانها ('میان' + plural suffix -ها) means 'their middles/waists' (literally 'the middles'); ازار here is a noun placed at the end of the clause (contextually a strap or the thing used to bind, lexically glossed as 'harm/annoyance' in the provided list but functioning here as the object associated with the binding). The sequence shows action (they bound them) followed by the spatial specification (around their middles) and the instrument/thing.
segments.s631-g27: همی is an adverb of continued or repeated action ('still/continually'); کرد is the simple past 'he did/made/exerted' (here 'exerted'); زور is the noun 'strength' and ان is the demonstrative 'that' referring back to the previously mentioned force. The phrase برین این بران is a paired locative/directional expression ('on that one, on this one' / 'this way and that') showing alternate application of the force; such stacked demonstratives are common in epic diction to indicate alternating action.
segments.s631-g28: گرازان ('boars') and پیچان ('twisting' or 'winding') are descriptive/figurative adjectives picturing the manner of action — violently twisting like boars; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; دو مرد گران is a noun phrase 'the two heavy/weighty men' (مرد = man/hero, گران = heavy/important). Grammatically this whole phrase functions as the affected entities of the preceding verb phrase (the two men who are thrown/twisted).
segments.s631-g29: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause; برداشت is the past-tense verb 'took up / lifted'; بهرام is the proper name subject; جام بلور is a noun phrase where جام ('cup') is modified postpositively by بلور ('crystal'), a common poetic order (noun + modifier). Together: 'When Bahram lifted the crystal cup...'.
segments.s631-g30: به introduces the goal/direction 'into' and is attached to مغزش (مغز 'brain' + ـش 'his') = 'into his brain/mind'; نبید is the noun 'wine'; اندرافگند is a past-tense verb meaning 'cast/put into' (classical compound of اندر + افگند); شور is the resulting state 'ardor/passion' and is the effect of the action. Word order places the directional phrase first, then the thing poured, then the verb, and finally the resulting condition.
segments.s631-g31: بشنگل is a proper name (the speaker); چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (in the line it marks direct speech); کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing a ruler, and شهریار is 'king' — together 'Beshengel thus said, “O king...”'. Note that classical Persian often uses چنین گفت to introduce quoted speech.
segments.s631-g32: بفرمای is an exhortation/imperative form asking the addressee to give an order ('please command' or 'order (it)'); تا here is the purpose marker 'so that/in order that'; من is the first-person pronoun 'I'; ببندم is the present-subjunctive/optative form 'that I may bind/tie' (بـ- prefix + بندم to show purpose or intention after تا); ازار is the object of ببندم (lexically 'harm/annoyance' in the provided senses, here likely the thing to be bound/tied). The clause is therefore a polite request to the king to give permission so the speaker can tie/bind something.

sentences.s631-s21: He struck him to the ground so violently that his bones were broken, and the color left his relaxed face.
sentences.s631-s22: He was left in a terrible daze, astonished at that lofty height and at that strength and the (sweeping) palm.
sentences.s631-s23: In the Indian tongue he called him 'Yazdān' and set him above forty men.
sentences.s631-s24: When they became drunk from the delightful wine, they left the jewel-decked iwan.
sentences.s631-s1: When Shangal heard, he said to Bahram that your counsel does not accord with the people.
sentences.s631-s2: Come down for a while and unloose the fastening; why do you speak such unprofitable words?
sentences.s631-s3: They built a single pleasant iwan, and everyone constructed whatever needed to be made.
sentences.s631-s4: Bahram did not rest until noon; when the bright crown of the world rose to its peak.
sentences.s631-s29: He ordered Bahram to mount, the Kayanian bow taken in his hand.
sentences.s631-s30: He said to Shengel thus: 'O king, know this — that the riders are with me.'
sentences.s631-s31: They would continually shoot arrows and play polo at will, whenever the magnanimous king gave the command.
sentences.s631-s32: Shengel said thus that the bow and arrows were the pillar of the riders, without doubt.
sentences.s631-s33: You, with horn and mane and with your hand kept low, lay your hand on the bowstring, string the bow, and spread your thumb.
sentences.s631-s34: Bahram the rider strung the bow and handed the reins to the war-steed.
sentences.s631-s35: One (of them) seized an arrow and opened his thumb; the mark struck a single post and broke together.
sentences.s631-s36: The horsemen of the field and the men of vengeance all cried out at once, 'Bravo!'
sentences.s631-s25: When the heavens put on Chinese silk, young and old alike were at rest from eating.
sentences.s631-s26: When that musk‑scented tent became golden, the radiant one displayed its face upon the sky.
sentences.s631-s27: The king of the Indians took his seat on the litter, and with a polo‑mallet in hand he strode into the field.
sentences.s631-s28: They carried (or brought) the king his arrows and bow; he rode/galloped on toward his desire for a moment.
sentences.s631-s9: When the bread had been eaten, they put the assembly in order; the player of the rud appeared and they wished (for music).
sentences.s631-s10: The scent of musk kept coming from the food beneath that very spread of gold-brocade.
sentences.s631-s11: When the great men grew merry from wine, their anxieties were undone and they became carefree.
sentences.s631-s12: He bade two men, strong champions, to test strength in wrestling against the demon-footed foe.
sentences.s631-s5: When they laid the banquet before Shangal, he ordered someone, "Who will sing?"
sentences.s631-s6: He is a Khosrow-worshipping envoy sent from Iran, a speaker and also a successful (fortunate) man.
sentences.s631-s7: Bring to the messengers' table anyone who is with him and who is saddled.
sentences.s631-s8: Bahram moved swiftly and sat at the table; he spread his fingertips and closed his lips.
sentences.s631-s17: When I grapple with the mighty, I will not become ruined or drunk.
sentences.s631-s18: Shangal laughed and said to him, 'Rise; when you bring them under you, spill their blood.'
sentences.s631-s19: When Bahram heard it, he rose to his feet; with manly strength he bent his upper body to the right.
sentences.s631-s20: He seized anyone from among them as if he were a lion that breaks forth into the living's grave.
sentences.s631-s13: The worthy men went off to their task and bound them around the middles with a strap.
sentences.s631-s14: He continued to exert that strength, throwing one this way and the other that way, twisting them like boars — the two heavy men.
sentences.s631-s15: When Bahram lifted the crystal cup, he poured wine into his brain and stirred up ardor.
sentences.s631-s16: Beshengel said thus: 'O king, command (it) so that I may bind the strap/annoyance.'
